activities_projects.title: Seasonal Migration Patterns
activities_projects.objective: Analyze the cyclical movements of Himalayan pastoralists
steps: Research the seasonal cycle of the Gujjar Bakarwals or the Gaddi shepherds[cite: 3].
Explain the climatic reasons for moving to 'bugyals' (high meadows) in summer and descending to 'bhabar' (dry forests) in winter[cite: 3].
Create a vertical timeline showing the months and corresponding altitudes for a single year.
Discuss how these movements help in the regeneration of pastures[cite: 3].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Analyzes the symbiotic relationship between climate, altitude, and pastoral survival.
activities_projects.evaluation: Accuracy of the seasonal timeline and explanation of pasture regeneration (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Colonial Policy and the 'Waste Land' Myth
activities_projects.objective: Evaluate the economic motivations behind colonial land laws
steps: Investigate the 'Waste Land Rules' and why the British viewed uncultivated grazing land as unproductive[cite: 3].
Analyze how transforming grazing land into cultivated farms increased state revenue[cite: 3].
Explain the impact of the Criminal Tribes Act on nomadic communities who were forced to settle in one place[cite: 3].
Discuss the long-term effects of these laws on the mobility of pastoralists[cite: 3].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Understands how colonial land revenue systems prioritized fixed agriculture over nomadic pastoralism.
activities_projects.evaluation: Depth of analysis regarding the link between land rules and revenue generation (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: The Plateau Interaction: Dhangars and Peasants
activities_projects.objective: Examine the exchange system between herders and settled farmers
steps: Map the migration of the Dhangars from the central plateau to the Konkan coast[cite: 3].
Identify the specific products exchanged between the Dhangars and the Konkani peasants (e.g., manure for grain)[cite: 3].
Explain why the Konkani peasants welcomed the shepherds at the end of the monsoon[cite: 3].
Discuss how the arrival of the monsoon dictates the departure of shepherds from the coast[cite: 3].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Recognizes the complex economic and ecological interdependence between different communities.
activities_projects.evaluation: Clarity of the interaction diagram and explanation of the exchange (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Closing the Commons: The Forest Acts
activities_projects.objective: Analyze the restriction of customary grazing rights in protected areas
steps: Compare how 'Reserved' and 'Protected' forest categories differently impacted pastoral entry[cite: 3].
Describe the permit system used by forest officials to control the number of cattle and duration of stay[cite: 3].
Discuss how the fixing of entry and exit dates led to overgrazing in permitted areas[cite: 3].
Evaluate the role of bribery and harassment by forest guards in the daily lives of herders[cite: 3].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Evaluates the transition from communal forest access to bureaucratic state control.
activities_projects.evaluation: Quality of the analysis on the consequences of the permit system (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Africa Case Study: The Loss of Maasai Grazing Lands
activities_projects.objective: Compare the impact of white settlement and game reserves on African pastoralists
steps: Locate the Maasai territory in Kenya and Tanzania[cite: 3].
Identify the portion of land lost to 'white settlers' and the creation of game reserves like the Serengeti[cite: 3].
Analyze why the best grazing lands were taken first, leaving pastoralists in arid, low-quality zones[cite: 3].
Discuss the phrase 'My People' in the context of their identity and land loss[cite: 3].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Identifies universal patterns of pastoral dispossession in colonial India and Africa.
activities_projects.evaluation: Comparison of land loss factors in India versus Africa (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Adaptation and Survival in the Modern World
activities_projects.objective: Examine how herders have responded to modern constraints
steps: Identify three specific ways pastoralists have adapted to reduced grazing grounds (e.g., changing routes, buying land)[cite: 3].
Explain why some Raikas changed their migration paths toward Haryana after the 1947 Partition[cite: 3].
Analyze the socio-economic shift where some pastoralists became settled traders while others became laborers[cite: 3].
Discuss the role of livestock in the modern market economy[cite: 3].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Recognizes the resilience and diverse survival strategies of modern pastoralists.
activities_projects.evaluation: Comprehensive list of adaptation strategies and their reasons (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Drought and its Disproportionate Impact
activities_projects.objective: Understand how limited mobility makes natural disasters worse
steps: Compare how pastoralists survived droughts in the past (by moving) versus the modern era (restricted by borders)[cite: 3].
Explain why the Maasai cattle population dropped significantly during the droughts of the 1930s[cite: 3].
Discuss the concept of 'overstocking' and its relation to restricted grazing areas[cite: 3].
Analyze the impact of drought on the 'poor' versus the 'rich' pastoralists[cite: 3].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Connects political boundaries to increased vulnerability during ecological crises.
activities_projects.evaluation: Depth of analysis on the vulnerability caused by restricted movement (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: The Grazing Tax: A Fiscal Burden
activities_projects.objective: Analyze the financial impact of colonial taxation on herders
steps: Identify when the Grazing Tax was introduced and how it was collected through contractors[cite: 3].
Explain the 'per head of cattle' payment system and the requirement of carrying a pass[cite: 3].
Calculate the potential financial burden on a family with a herd of 50 sheep[cite: 3].
Discuss how this tax influenced the decision to reduce herd sizes[cite: 3].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Understands taxation as a method of state surveillance and control over nomads.
activities_projects.evaluation: Accuracy of calculations and understanding of the tax's social impact (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Social Hierarchy within Pastoral Societies
activities_projects.objective: Examine the internal divisions between the rich and poor herders
steps: Identify the 'elders' and the 'warriors' in Maasai society and their traditional roles[cite: 3].
Explain how colonial appointments of 'chiefs' disrupted these traditional hierarchies[cite: 3].
Analyze why 'rich' pastoralists could survive losses by lending money, while 'poor' pastoralists became laborers[cite: 3].
Discuss the widening gap between different sections of the pastoral community[cite: 3].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Analyzes how external political interventions reshape internal social structures.
activities_projects.evaluation: Clear breakdown of traditional vs. colonial-imposed social hierarchies (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Pastoralism vs. Conservation
activities_projects.objective: Critique the exclusion of herders from modern wildlife parks
steps: Research how the creation of the Samburu or Amboseli National Parks affected the Maasai[cite: 3].
List the arguments used by governments to justify the ban on grazing in these areas[cite: 3].
Discuss the irony where 'preservation' of nature leads to the destruction of a nature-dependent lifestyle[cite: 3].
Evaluate if modern ecotourism benefits the local pastoral communities[cite: 3].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Critically evaluates the cost of modern conservation on indigenous livelihoods.
activities_projects.evaluation: Quality of the ethical argument regarding conservation and rights (10 marks)
